title: “Deepening Our Practice of Meditation” | A Talk by YSS Sannyasi Swami Smaranananda Giri
formattedMarkdown:
# “Deepening Our Practice of Meditation”

### Swami Smaranananda Giri • Yogoda Satsanga Society of India

My dear friends, today let us contemplate on how we can deepen, sweeten, and enrich our daily practice of meditation. Many spiritual seekers start their journey with great enthusiasm, yet after a few months or years, they find their practice becoming dry, mechanical, or routine. This is a common phase, but it is one that we must consciously transcend to experience the true nectar of the soul.

Meditation is not a passive mental state, nor is it a chore. Meditation is an active, loving communion with the Divine. It is the art of stilling the storm of thoughts on the lake of our mind, so that we can clearly see the reflection of the Divine Moon.

As our beloved Guru Paramahansa Yogananda frequently emphasized:
> "Silence is the altar of Spirit."

To build this altar, we must understand the spiritual principles that govern deep meditation and apply them with perseverance and devotion.

---

### Overcoming Restlessness and Breath-Control

The primary obstacle to deep meditation is physical and mental restlessness. When we sit to meditate, we notice that the mind runs in a thousand directions like a wild horse. This restless thinking is directly linked to our breath.

There is a strict, mathematical relationship between the state of our respiration and the state of our mind. When you are angry, fearful, or excited, your breath is shallow and rapid. When you are calm, deeply concentrated, or asleep, your breath becomes naturally slow and peaceful.

Therefore, by scientifically centering our attention on the breath, we can calm the mind. Through the practice of the Hong-Sau technique, which Yoganandaji called the "greatest key to concentration," we learn to observe the natural inhalation and exhalation without forcing it.

As you sit in meditation:
1. Keep your posture tall and straight. A bent spine pinches the energy flow and makes the mind sluggish.
2. Relax your body completely. Any physical tension acts as a lightning rod, grounding your energy in body-consciousness.
3. Turn your gaze upward to the point between the eyebrows (the spiritual eye). This is the seat of divine concentration and ecstasy.
4. Observe the breath as an impartial witness. As the heart rate slows and respiration calms, the energy naturally withdraws from the outer senses and moves upward into the brain, filling you with a profound sense of peace.

---

### The Nectar of Devotion (Bhakti)

Scientific techniques are essential; they are the tracks upon which the train of meditation runs. But tracks alone cannot move a train. The fuel that powers the train is **devotion (Bhakti)**.

Techniques without devotion are like dry bones. Devotion is the flesh and blood that makes the practice alive. Our Guru taught that you must talk to God in the language of your own heart. Speak to Him as your Father, Mother, Friend, or beloved Guru.

At the end of your techniques, drop all efforts and sit in the silence. It is in this post-meditation period that the real treasure is found. Do not immediately stand up when your techniques are finished. Sit quietly for ten, fifteen, or thirty minutes, absorbed in the peace, light, or love that you feel.

This quiet waiting—with deep love and expectancy—is where the soul begins to taste the nectar of the Infinite.

---

### Practical Tips for Daily Living

To keep your meditation fresh and deep, remember these key habits:

* **Set Apart a Sacred Space:** Dedicate a small corner of your room solely for meditation. Over time, that spot becomes highly magnetized with peace and spiritual energy. When you sit there, you will find it automatically easier to go deep.
* **Quality Over Quantity:** It is far better to meditate for 10 minutes with intense concentration and deep feeling than to sit for an hour with your mind wandering to your groceries or work emails.
* **Maintain the peace throughout the day:** After finishing your morning meditation, try to hold onto that sense of still peace as you go about your duties. Act with calm efficiency, keeping a mental stream of remembrance always active.

May your meditations grow sweeter and deeper each day, and may you feel the quiet embrace of the Divine Presence always. Jai Guru.
